title: Ellipsis and substitution
lesson: Advanced cohesion avoids repetition by substituting a short word for a longer chunk. 'So' substitutes for a positive 'that'-clause after verbs of thinking and saying (think, hope, suppose, be afraid, expect): 'Is she coming?' 'I think so.' To negate, the choice of pattern is verb-specific: 'hope', 'be afraid', 'guess', 'assume', 'presume', 'suspect' take a positive verb + 'not' ('I hope not'), whereas 'think', 'believe', 'expect', 'imagine' take a negative verb + 'so' ('I don't think so'). 'Do so' is a formal substitute for a previously mentioned action ('She agreed to sign and did so at once'); plain 'do' / an auxiliary alone is the everyday option. 'One/ones' replaces a countable noun ('the red one'). After a comparison or 'such', use 'such' for a whole category. To echo agreement, 'so'/'neither'/'nor' + auxiliary + subject inverts ('So do I', 'Neither can he').

items.text: "Will the strike go ahead?" "I'm afraid {1} — talks broke down last night."

blanks.hint: substitute for the positive clause 'the strike will go ahead'

items.explanation: 'So' substitutes for the affirmative 'that'-clause ('the strike will go ahead'), which is what 'I'm afraid' confirms here. 'I'm afraid not' would deny it (i.e. no strike), the opposite of what the broken-down talks imply; bare 'it' cannot substitute for a clause after 'afraid'.

items.text: "Has the committee reached a decision yet?" "I don't {1} so — they're still deliberating."

blanks.hint: which verb pairs with a NEGATIVE verb + 'so', not 'verb + not'?

items.explanation: 'Think', 'believe', 'expect' and 'imagine' negate as 'don't think so'. 'Hope' and 'guess' do not take 'don't ... so'; they form the negative the other way ('I hope not', 'I guess not'), so 'don't hope so' / 'don't guess so' are not idiomatic.

items.text: She promised to apologise to the team, and she {1} the moment the meeting opened.

blanks.hint: formal substitute for the action 'apologise to the team'

items.explanation: 'Do so' is the standard pro-form substituting for a whole verb phrase ('apologise to the team'). 'Make so' is not an English substitution form, and 'did it so' is ungrammatical because 'do so' already contains the object reference — you cannot add 'it'.

items.text: These gloves are too thin for winter; I need to find some warmer {1}.

blanks.hint: substitutes for a plural countable noun ('gloves')

items.explanation: 'Ones' substitutes for a countable plural noun after an adjective ('warmer ones' = 'warmer gloves'). 'Such' would replace a category, not a counted noun after 'warmer', and 'so' substitutes for a clause, not a noun.

items.text: The first proposal was rejected outright, and {1} the second.

blanks.hint: echo a POSITIVE statement with inversion: '___ + aux + subject'

items.explanation: The first clause is affirmative ('was rejected'), so the additive echo uses 'so' + the same auxiliary ('was') + subject: 'so was the second'. 'Neither' is for echoing a negative, and 'so did' would need a 'do'-auxiliary that the passive 'was rejected' does not use.

items.text: The lecture was so dull that several students dozed off, and the seminar afterwards was {1} tedious.

blanks.hint: compares the seminar's tedium to the lecture's: '___ tedious'

items.explanation: 'Just as' before the adjective draws an equal comparison ('just as tedious' = equally tedious as the dull lecture). 'So as' introduces purpose before an infinitive, not a comparison before an adjective, and 'much so' is not a grammatical pre-modifier of 'tedious' here.

items.text: "Do you think it will rain before the ceremony?" "I hope {1} — the marquee still isn't up."

blanks.hint: 'hope' forms its negative substitute as 'verb + ___', not 'don't ... so'

items.explanation: 'Hope' negates a substituted clause as 'hope not' (= I hope it will not rain), the sense required since the speaker is anxious about the unfinished marquee. 'Hope so' would mean the opposite (hoping for rain), and 'no' is not a clause substitute after 'hope'.

items.text: He had never set foot in the building before, and {1} had any of his colleagues.

blanks.hint: echo a NEGATIVE statement with inversion: '___ + aux + subject'

items.explanation: The first clause is negative ('had never set foot'), so the additive echo uses 'neither' + auxiliary + subject ('neither had any of his colleagues'). 'So' echoes an affirmative, not a negative, and 'either' needs a preceding 'nor/not' and does not invert this way.

items.text: Applicants may submit a portfolio or a written essay; {1} submission must reach us by Friday.

blanks.hint: stands for 'whichever of the two' before the singular noun 'submission'

items.explanation: 'Either' substitutes for 'one or the other of the two' options just listed and correctly precedes the singular 'submission'. 'Such' would need an article ('such a submission') and substitutes for a whole category rather than picking one of two, and 'the former' would single out only the portfolio, contradicting 'or'.

items.text: The report praised both the engineers and the planners, but it was {1} who had warned of the delay months earlier.

blanks.hint: refers back to the SECOND of the two groups mentioned ('the planners')

items.explanation: 'The latter' substitutes for the second-named of two items — here 'the planners', the group that gave the warning. 'The former' would point to the engineers (the first group), and 'such' substitutes for a category, not for one of two previously named groups.

items.text: The climate here is far milder than {1} of the highlands further north.

blanks.hint: substitutes for the uncountable noun 'the climate' before the post-modifier 'of...'

items.explanation: 'That of' substitutes for a singular or uncountable noun followed by a defining 'of'-phrase ('that of the highlands' = 'the climate of the highlands'). 'Such of' is not a substitution pattern here, and 'one' cannot substitute for an uncountable noun like 'climate' — 'one/ones' replaces countable nouns only.

items.text: Check whether the funding has been approved, and {1} release the first instalment at once.

blanks.hint: substitutes a whole condition: 'if it has (been approved)'

items.explanation: 'If so' is a pro-form condition standing for the affirmative version of the preceding clause ('if the funding has been approved'). 'So as' introduces purpose before a to-infinitive ('so as to release'), and 'so that' introduces a finite purpose/result clause; neither substitutes for an implied condition the way 'if so' does.

items.text: I never expected the scheme to succeed, yet succeed it {1}, against all the odds.

blanks.hint: a bare 'do'-auxiliary substitutes for the fronted verb 'succeed' in this emphatic inversion

items.explanation: When a verb is fronted for emphasis ('succeed it ___'), a bare auxiliary 'did' stands in for the lexical verb 'succeed'. 'Did so' is the pro-form for a full verb phrase and is redundant once 'succeed' has already been stated and fronted; bare 'so' cannot carry the past tense or close this inverted clause.

items.text: Some delegates backed the amendment with genuine enthusiasm, others {1} only reluctantly.

blanks.hint: an auxiliary substitutes for the verb phrase 'backed the amendment'; match the past simple tense

items.explanation: The verb phrase 'backed the amendment' is past simple, so the substitute auxiliary is the 'do'-operator in the past, 'did' ('others did only reluctantly'). 'Were' would require a passive or continuous original ('were backing'), and 'had' would require a perfect form ('had backed'), neither of which matches the plain past simple 'backed'.
